source_label: Mastering Lightning Network — Chapter 9: Channel Operation and Payment Forwarding
excerpt: The to_self_delay parameter sets how long the broadcasting party must wait before they can spend their own output from a force-closed commitment. This window is the time the counterparty has to detect a breach and broadcast a justice transaction. Watchtowers are the standard way to make that detection work even when the counterparty is offline.
body:
Source excerpt:
The to_self_delay parameter sets how long the broadcasting party must wait before they can spend their own output from a force-closed commitment. This window is the time the counterparty has to detect a breach and broadcast a justice transaction. Watchtowers are the standard way to make that detection work even when the counterparty is offline.

---

Cited by leaf "Watchtowers and the offline-channel problem":
The Lightning security model assumes that whenever your channel counterparty broadcasts an old (revoked) commitment transaction, you have time to broadcast a *justice transaction* that uses the revocation secret to sweep both your output and theirs as a penalty. This works perfectly if you are online and watching the chain. It fails completely if you are offline for longer than your channel's `to_self_delay` window — typically a day or two for retail channels — because by then your counterparty's stolen state will have matured past the timelock and they can spend it freely [1].

A *watchtower* is a third party that watches the chain on your behalf and broadcasts the justice transaction if a revoked commitment ever surfaces. The trick is doing this without giving the watchtower custody of your funds or visibility into your channel balance. BOLT-13 (in draft status) and the Eye of Satoshi specification define a *blind* watchtower protocol: for each new channel state, you send the watchtower a small encrypted blob keyed by a hint derived from the txid of the corresponding revoked commitment. The watchtower stores the blob without being able to read it. When that commitment hits the chain, the watchtower derives the same hint, looks up the blob, decrypts it using a key reconstructed from the chain data, and uses it to broadcast a pre-signed justice transaction whose output pays the watchtower's reward and the rest back to you [2].

Crucially, the watchtower never holds your private keys, never sees your channel balance, and never learns which of your channels triggered a justice transaction (each blob looks identical until the chain reveals which one matters). The worst a malicious watchtower can do is fail to act — at which point you are no worse off than if you had no watchtower, because you would still only lose funds in the specific case where you were offline at the moment a breach happened. The standard mitigation against a single-point-of-failure tower is to subscribe to multiple independent watchtowers; the cost per watch-update is a few sats per state change.

Watchtowers exist because Lightning's punishment mechanism is asymmetric in *time*, not in capability. Both parties can punish, but only the one who is watching when a breach happens. Mobile-only and intermittent-internet users — most of the world — are the population this matters for. Self-custodial mobile wallets like Phoenix, Breez, and Zeus integrate watchtower clients by default, often paid as a small subscription to a node operator running The Eye of Satoshi or `rust-teos`.

A separate but related question is *eltoo* (BOLT-eltoo, requires the SIGHASH_ANYPREVOUT softfork), an alternative channel construction in which any later state always supersedes any earlier one without needing punishment at all. If activated, eltoo would remove the watchtower-or-be-robbed asymmetry entirely and let channels recover from breach attempts on their own. Until ANYPREVOUT activates, watchtowers are the production answer for offline channels — and the reason Lightning works at all on a phone that's frequently in someone's pocket.
